fix_plans.title: Swap AdamW for LBFGS on convex LR
fix_plans.content: Replace the AdamW optimizer in train.py with torch.optim.LBFGS. With only ONE linear layer on L2-regularised sparse TF-IDF features, the loss surface is convex — AdamW's stochastic noisy updates in 20 epochs (256-batch mini-steps, lr=1e-3) leave the model far short of the true global optimum. The 20% recall gap plateau in probe_result_2.json from epoch 7 through 20 is exactly the signature of an AdamW run that has hit a noisy flat-spot without having actually converged: the weights stop meaningfully changing but the per-group TPRs remain misaligned. LBFGS (full-batch or at least full-epoch deterministic, since the seed-pinned loader is already deterministic) closes this gap in a single optimisation pass. Concretely: in train.py lines 318-320 replace `torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=LEARNING_RATE, weight_decay=WEIGHT_DECAY)` with `torch.optim.LBFGS(model.parameters(), lr=1.0, max_iter=1, max_eval=None, tolerance_grad=1e-9, tolerance_change=1e-11, history_size=100, line_search_fn='strong_wolfe')`. Then refactor train_one_epoch to wrap its closure in the LBFGS closure pattern (return loss after a single full pass, call optimizer.step(closure)). Because LBFGS does its own line search, the outer `for epoch` loop can remain at NUM_EPOCHS=20 — each epoch becomes one LBFGS iteration which internally does many gradient evaluations. Keep WEIGHT_DECAY=1e-4 (passed via weight_decay=WEIGHT_DECAY to LBFGS). Expected effect: the model moves much closer to the true minimum of the weighted BCE+L2 objective, which for sparse TF-IDF with ~9% positives pushes each per-group logit toward a calibrated probability that recovers the majority of positives at the fixed 0.5 threshold, closing the tail on the minority groups that currently freeze at low recall.

fix_plans.title: Add batch-norm + dropout feature conditioner
fix_plans.content: Add a small feature-conditioning block between input and the linear logit layer inside the LogisticRegression class. TF-IDF vectors are sparse, heavy-tailed and very high-dimensional; a single nn.Linear maps them directly to a single logit with no chance to normalise per-token scales or to regularise the noisy non-zero positions. This causes logit magnitudes to vary wildly across groups whose text-token distributions differ (e.g. black vs. white cohorts have subtly different clinical-note token distributions), so their sigmoid outputs cross the frozen 0.5 threshold at very different rates — the root of the 20% recall gap. Concrete change in train.py: replace `class LogisticRegression` (lines 89-97) with a version that adds `self.bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(input_dim, affine=False, eps=1e-6)` and `self.drop = nn.Dropout(0.3)`; in forward, compute `x = self.bn(x); x = self.drop(x); return self.linear(x).squeeze(-1)`. Set affine=False because the TF-IDF features have meaningful semantic scales that shouldn't be relearned — just remove mean / unit-variance per token across the batch. Train-time BN provides stochastic regularisation that tightens the per-group probability distribution; dropout(0.3) on the sparse non-zeros prevents the single linear layer from overfitting to the rare positive tokens that happen to cluster in one group. No other file changes. Expected: smaller inter-group logit spread -> per-group TPRs converge closer together at threshold 0.5.

fix_plans.title: Stratified minority bootstrap in training loader
fix_plans.content: Replace the plain `DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True)` construction in train.py lines 305 with a custom sampler that oversamples the 9% positive class AND the minority ethnicity groups during EACH epoch, so every 256-sample mini-batch contains a guaranteed count of positive examples AND a guaranteed count of samples from the smaller ethnicity groups (black, hispanic, asian). Concretely: after loading train_ds, build an index `pos_eth_idx = [i for i in range(len(train_ds)) if train_ds.labels[i] > 0.5]` and `eth_counts = {g: sum(train_ds.eth[i][g] > 0.5 for i in pos_eth_idx + neg_idx) for g in ETH_NAMES}` to learn the minimum minority-group support. Construct per-sample weights `w_i = (1/n_tfidf) * (pos_weight if label_i==1 else 1.0) * (minority_boost if eth_i in {black,hispanic,asian} else 1.0)` where minority_boost = 2.0. Use `torch.utils.data.WeightedRandomSampler(weights=w, num_samples=len(train_ds), replacement=True)` instead of shuffle=True on the train_loader. Keep the seeded torch.Generator as the sampler's generator so the bootstrap is deterministic between runs. This is NOT changing the loss or the model — it's changing the exposure rate of the gradients to minority + positive samples so LogisticRegression's single weight vector can actually see enough minority-group death examples to pull their logit up to the 0.5 threshold. The probe then measures at the SAME frozen threshold (which it is not allowed to move) but the model has learned to cross it more equally across groups. Expected: minority-group recall rises toward majority recall, shrinking the gap from 20% toward <=10%.
